title: Manual Handling and Workplace Musculoskeletal Injury
synonyms: Manual Handling and Workplace Musculoskeletal Injury

sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Manual handling injuries often involve a combination of load, posture, repetition, fatigue, terrain, and recovery failure. Assessment should look beyond the single incident to the full task system.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain linked to lifting, carrying, pushing, pulling, awkward reach, or repetitive handling
- Back, shoulder, neck, or limb symptoms during or after tasks
- Report that the task felt heavier, faster, or more awkward than usual
- Recurrence in the same job or shift pattern

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Heavy load is only one risk factor; poor access, twisting, unstable footing, heat, fatigue, and time pressure also matter
- Serious injury red flags still need to be screened before treating the case as a routine strain
- A handling incident may reveal broader system failures that need workplace action

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Clarify the task, immediate symptoms, and whether the worker can safely continue any duties
- Remove or modify aggravating tasks early
- Escalate if symptoms suggest fracture, neurological deficit, major trauma, or acute medical illness

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Analyse the task demands, posture, repetition, load, environment, equipment, and recovery opportunity
- Recommend risk-reduction strategies such as team lift, mechanical aid, route change, height adjustment, or task redesign
- Build a graded recovery plan that restores handling tolerance without abrupt exposure to the same failing task

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Good injury management often requires workplace redesign as much as individual rehabilitation
